coredata.dc:title: A nanocomposite optosensor of hydroxyapatite and graphene quantum dots embedded within highly specific polymer for norfloxacin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Bunkoed, Opas
Donkhampa, Pajaree
Nurerk, Piyaluk
coredata.dc:description: A nanocomposite fluorescent probe was developed for the detection of norfloxacin. The fluorescent nanoprobe was fabricated by incorporating hydroxyapatite and graphene quantum dots into a highly selective polymer. The fabrication procedure, detection system and analytical performance of the nanoprobe were optimized for norfloxacin detection. Quenching of the emission intensity of the fluorescent nanoprobe was linear from 1.0 to 100.0 μg L−1 of norfloxacin. The limit of detection was 0.35 μg L−1. The fluorescent nanoprobe exhibited an excellent specificity for norfloxacin with a high imprinting factor of 68.7. Applied to detect norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk, the optosensor achieved recoveries between 93.8 and 99.3%. Precision was good with RSD below 6%. Hydroxyapatite enhanced the adsorption of norfloxacin on the probe, graphene quantum dots improved the sensitivity of the sensing response and the molecularly imprinted polymer increased the specificity of the probe. The fabricated optosensor was simple to prepare and detection was rapid with excellent sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Norfloxacin
Graphene quantum dots
Hydroxyapatite
Nanocomposite fluorescent probe
Molecularly imprinted polymer

Sample preparation of milk and chicken meat
Characterization of GQDs and HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe
Ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
Comparison of different probes for norfloxacin detection
Analytical performance of nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe
Selectivity of HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe for norfloxacin detection
Analysis of norfloxacin in food samples
The determination of norfloxacin using optosensing and other methods

The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English assistance. This research was also supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship from Prince of Songkla University.

This work was supported by Prince of Songkla University ( SCI6202115N-0 ), Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English assistance. This research was also supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship from Prince of Songkla University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe for norfloxacin detection.
TEM image of GQDs (A), size distribution histogram of GQDs (B), SEM image of HA (C) and nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe (D).
Effect of ratio of template (T) to monomer (M) and cross-linker (C) for the fabrication of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescence probe.
The sensitivity of different fluorescent probes for the detection of norfloxacin at concentrations from 1.0 to 100.0 μg L−1.

Emission spectra of nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe after incubation with different concentrations of norfloxacin (1.0–100 μg L−1) (A) and the calibration curve of nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP and HA-GQDs-NIP fluorescent probes for norfloxacin (B).
The selectivity of the developed nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe for norfloxacin detection (A) and the results of a study of competitive binding between norfloxacin and ciprofloxacin (B).
The determination of norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk samples.
Comparison of the developed optosensor with other methods for the detection of norfloxacin.

head.ce:title: A nanocomposite optosensor of hydroxyapatite and graphene quantum dots embedded within highly specific polymer for norfloxacin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90112, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90112, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanocomposite fluorescent probe was developed for the detection of norfloxacin. The fluorescent nanoprobe was fabricated by incorporating hydroxyapatite and graphene quantum dots into a highly selective polymer. The fabrication procedure, detection system and analytical performance of the nanoprobe were optimized for norfloxacin detection. Quenching of the emission intensity of the fluorescent nanoprobe was linear from 1.0 to 100.0 μg L−1 of norfloxacin. The limit of detection was 0.35 μg L−1. The fluorescent nanoprobe exhibited an excellent specificity for norfloxacin with a high imprinting factor of 68.7. Applied to detect norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk, the optosensor achieved recoveries between 93.8 and 99.3%. Precision was good with RSD below 6%. Hydroxyapatite enhanced the adsorption of norfloxacin on the probe, graphene quantum dots improved the sensitivity of the sensing response and the molecularly imprinted polymer increased the specificity of the probe. The fabricated optosensor was simple to prepare and detection was rapid with excellent sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Norfloxacin is one of the group of broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotics widely utilized in the treatment of infectious diseases of the urinary, respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts in humans and food-producing animals [1,2]. It chemical structure as demonstrated in Fig. S1. However, overuse of norfloxacin may leave its residues in livestock products. These residues can cause drowsiness, anorexia, dizziness and nausea in people who consume these products [3,4]. Long-term consumption of residual norfloxacin can cause disorders of the central nervous system and induce antibiotic resistance [5]. Thus, it is necessary to develop a reliable method for monitoring of norfloxacin in livestock products. Norfloxacin has been determined by chromatography [6–8], electrophoresis [9,10] and electrochemistry [11,12]. However, these methods may entail complicated processes, the use of large amounts of reagents, long analysis time and expensive instruments. Spectrofluorometry is an interesting, highly sensitive technique for the detection of norfloxacin. The technique is simple in operation and provides rapid detection with a minimal consumption of reagents and low-cost equipment [13,14]. In addition, analytical performance of detection can be improved using a fluorescent probe with the suitable optical property, affinity and specificity [15].

In recent years, inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), a type of photosensitive nanomaterial, have been applied as fluorescent probes due to their outstanding optical properties [16,17]. Nevertheless, most inorganic QDs contain heavy metals and the potential toxicity of heavy metals toward the environment and living organisms have constrained their application [18]. More recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of environmentally friendly carbon dots, have gained considerable attention owing to their advantages of low toxicity, excellent photoluminescence, photobleaching resistance and ease of preparation [19,20]. These superior properties of GQDs made them promising candidates for application in catalysts [21], biomedicine [22] and sensors [22,23]. One of the great challenges of fluorescent sensing applications, is the development of highly sensitive fluorescent probes which can determine the target analyte at trace levels even in highly complex matrices.

One approach to the improvement of the selectivity of fluorescent probes is the use of molecular imprinting to obtain specific recognition cavities of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) materials. MIPs are generally synthesized via co-polymerization in the presence of functional monomers, which form complexes with the target analyte as template molecules, and cross-linkers [24]. The subsequent removal of the template molecules leaves specific recognition cavities which are complementary to the target analyte in shape, size and chemical functionality [25]. Moreover, MIPs also have good chemical and thermal stability while being easy to prepare [26]. The combination of the high specificity of MIPs and the good optical properties of GQDs has accelerated the development of composite fluorescent probes to detect organic compounds such as dopamine [27], metronidazole [28] and paranitrophenol [29]. Furthermore, the adsorption affinity of norfloxacin for the composite fluorescent probe can be improved by the addition of hydroxyapatite (HA) during synthesis of the probe. HA consists of hydroxyl groups which contributed to hydrogen bonding with the target analyte [30,31]. In addition, HA exhibited useful properties, including high surface area, non-toxicity, good stability and ease of synthesis [32].

In this work, a nanocomposite fluorescent probe of MIP composited with HA and GQDs was synthesized for norfloxacin detection. The detection of norfloxacin was based on fluorescence quenching when the target analytes were recognized at the specific binding sites of the nanocomposite probe. The integration of the optical properties of GQDs, high specificity of MIP and high adsorption ability of HA could improve the sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity of the optosensor. The fabricated nanoprobe was applied to detect norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk.

Citric acid and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Norfloxacin was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Sodium hydrogen orthophosphate and disodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate, sodium hydroxide, ethanol and ammonia were purchased from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand).

Scanning electron microscopy (JSM-5200) (JEOL, Japan) was used to observe the morphology of the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe. SEM images were recorded at the operating voltage of 25 kV. The Avaspec 2048 spectrometer (Avantes, Netherlands) was used to measure the absorption of norfloxacin and the fluorescent probe. The RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for fluorescence measurement. Fluorescence intensity was measured by setting the slit widths at 10 nm for the excitation and emission. The BX FTIR spectroscope (PerkinElmer, USA) was used to investigate the functional groups of the fluorescent probe.

The procedure for the synthesis of hydroxyapatite (HA) was carried out according to a previous works with some modification as follows [33]: under stirring for 1.0 h at 60 °C, 250 mL of Ca(OH)2 (0.1 M) was added dropwise to 0.10 M H3PO4 to obtain a Ca/P ratio of 1.67. The pH was adjusted to 11.0 by adding 1.0 M NaOH. Stirring of the suspension continued for 3 h. The synthesized hydroxyapatite was then filtered and washed three times with 20 mL of ethanol and 50 mL of deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and nanocomposite fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized as follows: 2.0 g of citric acid was heated at 250 °C using heating mantle until the solution became pale yellow. The solution was then added into 100 mL of NaOH (0.25 M) and stirred for 20 min. The obtained liquid was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (12 kDa cut off) for 24 h. The synthesized GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C until used.

To obtain the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe, 5.0 mL of APTES was added into a 20 mL solution of GQDs and the mixture was incubated under magnetic stirring for 30 min at 40 °C. At the same time, 10 mg of norfloxacin and 10.0 mg of the synthesized hydroxyapatite were dispersed in 5.0 mL ethanol (mixture B) to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then, mixture A was mixed with mixture B and stirred for 30 min, template interacted with functional monomer at this step, after which 420 μL of TEOS and 1.0 mL of NH3 were added. The mixture was aged for another 6 h. Finally, to obtain nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probes the template molecule (norfloxacin) was removed from the composite fluorescent probes by washing four times with ethanol (20 mL). The synthesized nanocomposite probes were kept as powder in a desiccator until used. HA-GQDs-NIP probes were also prepared under the same conditions as the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe but without adding the norfloxacin template. Fig. 1 illustrates the fabrication procedure of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probes.

To detect the analyte of interest, the nanocomposite probe was dispersed in phosphate buffer at pH 8.0. The fluorescence quenching of 150 μL of HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe was measured in 50 μL of quencher (norfloxacin or sample) solution. The mixture was incubated at ambient temperature (25 ± 1 °C) for 8 min. Fluorescence intensity was measured and recorded with slit width of 10 nm, an emission range of 400 to 700 nm, and an excitation of 355 nm. The measurement of fluorescence emission was carried out with and without norfloxacin and recorded as F and F0, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation of milk and chicken meat
ce:section.ce:para: The pretreatment of milk was modified from a previous work as follows [29]: 5.0 mL of milk was mixed with 15 mL of acetonitrile in a polypropylene tube and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and evaporated at 60 °C. The extractant was dissolved in 2.0 mL of phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) and 50 μL was mixed with 150 μL of HA-GQDs-MIP solution for the detection of norfloxacin.

The pretreatment of chicken meat was modified from a previous work as follows [24]: 5.0 mL of ethanol and 1.0 g of chicken meat were placed in a polypropylene tube and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred to another tube, mixed with 2.0 mL hexane, and centrifuged for 5 min to remove fat. The lower phase (ethanol) was evaporated at 60 °C, dissolved in 2.0 mL phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) and 50 μL of the sample solution were mixed with 150 μL of HA-GQDs-MIP solution for the analysis of norfloxacin. The accuracy of the developed optosensor was evaluated in term of recovery (%) by spiking norfloxacin in milk and chicken meat at four different concentrations. The recovery (%) was obtained from the following equation:
Recovery
%
=
C found
-
C sample
/
C added
× 100
where, Cfound is concentration of norfloxacin after adding a known amount of standard, Csample is concentration of norfloxacin in sample and Cadded is concentration of standard spiked in real sample.

The determination of norfloxacin by HPLC was performed using the Hewlett-Packard 1100 series (Agilent Technologies, Waldbronn, Germany) equipped with a Fortis C18 (4.6 mm × 15 cm, 5 μm) column. The mobile phase was a mixture of 25 mM of phosphoric acid (80%) and acetonitrile (20%) and the flow rate of the mobile phase was controlled at 0.9 mL min−1. Norfloxacin was detected by the fluorescence detector at excitation of 280 nm and emission of 450 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the synthesized GQD nanoparticles was investigated using TEM (Fig. 2 A). The TEM image shows spherical GQDs with a particle size of about 12 nm. A histogram of the particle size distribution is shown in Fig. 2B. The morphology of the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe was investigated by SEM (Fig. 2C). The SEM image shows spherical HA-GQDs-MIP probes with a diameter of about 300 nm (Fig. 2D). The nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP probe was synthesized via copolymerization between norfloxacin, APTES, and TEOS which were template, monomer, and cross-linker, respectively. HA, APTES, norfloxacin and GQDs bonded via hydrogen bonding. The composite probe was formed when the cross-linker and a catalyst of ammonia were added into the mixture. Synthesis occurred in a one-step copolymerization at ambient temperature (27 ± 2 °C).

The fabrication of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe was also characterized by FTIR. The FTIR spectrum of GQDs exhibited stretching vibrations of CO and –OH at 1586 and 3440 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1150 and 2930 cm−1 were due to C–O and –CH2 stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. S2a). The spectrum of HA showed bending vibrations of the carbonate group at 869 and 1461 cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 570 and 1041 corresponded to stretching vibrations of the phosphate group. The broad band around 3450 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group (–OH) (Fig. S2b). The synthesized HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe showed stretching and bending vibrations of the same specific groups as HA and GQDs (Fig. S2c). These results confirmed that GQDs and HA were embedded in the MIP.

The fluorescence emission spectrum of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP probe in the presence of the template molecule (norfloxacin) exhibited a low intensity (Fig. S3a). After template removal (Fig. S3b), emission intensity was restored to the same level as the non-imprinted polymer (Fig. S3c). The nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe before and after template removal is shown in Fig. S3d and Fig. S3e. These observations confirmed that the template molecule was completely removed from the nanocomposite fluorescent probe which was then ready to detect norfloxacin in real samples.

To obtain the best sensing performance from the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe, the incubation time, pH of the probe dispersion solution and molar ratio of template to functional monomer to cross-linker were investigated and optimized.

The incubation time for binding between recognition sites on the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe and norfloxacin was varied from 1 to 30 min. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of the probe increased until 8 min and then remained rather stable (Fig. S4A). This result implied that after 8 min binding between HA-GQDs-MIP and norfloxacin was complete. Thus, 8 min was selected as the most appropriate incubation time.

One of the significant parameters affecting the sensitivity of the probe was the pH of the dispersion solution containing the nanocomposite probes. The pH of the dispersion solution affected hydrogen bonding between norfloxacin and binding sites. The sensitivity was investigated of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP probe dispersed in phosphate buffer at pH 5.0 to 9.0. The highest sensitivity was achieved at pH 8.0 (Fig. S4B). Sensitivity decreased when pH was lower than 8.0 owing to the protonation of the –NH2 group of the functional monomer and norfloxacin. Sensitivity also decreased above pH 8.0 because of the ionization of the silica layer and the deprotonation of the –COOH group of norfloxacin. Consequently, the optimal pH of the nanocomposite probe dispersion solution for the detection of norfloxacin was 8.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of template (T) to functional monomer (M) and cross-linker (C) usually affects the morphology, chemical and physical properties, and performance of fluorescent probes. In this work, the ratio of T:M:C of 1:50:75 provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 3 ). When the ratio of monomer was low, there was a low number of functional groups (NH2) on the surface of the nanocomposite probe and sensitivity was low. However, when the ratio of monomer was too high, non-specific sites were formed in the polymer layer that inhibited binding between norfloxacin and specific sites, reducing sensitivity. Inappropriate quantities of cross linker also resulted in low sensitivity. At low ratios of cross-linker, GQDs, template and monomer were easily unbound, producing a structurally weak nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP. On the other hand, excess cross linker produced a rigid nanocomposite structure that obstructed interaction with the template. The ratio of T: M: C of 1:50:75 was chosen for the fabrication of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different probes for norfloxacin detection
ce:section.ce:para: Nanocomposite GQDs-NIP, HA-GQDs-NIP, GQDs-MIP and HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probes were investigated for the detection of norfloxacin. The HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 4 ) due to the many specific recognition cavities for norfloxacin in the MIP layer. HA also helped to improve adsorption affinity between norfloxacin and the fluorescent probe. GQDs-NIP exhibited the lowest sensitivity for norfloxacin detection because there were no specific cavities on the surface of the non-imprinted polymer. The results also showed that the presence of HA in both HA-GQDs-NIP and HA-GQDs-MIP improved sensitivity of norfloxacin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe for norfloxacin detection was assessed under the optimized conditions. The fluorescence emission of the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe decreased with the increasing concentration of norfloxacin (Fig. 5 A). The signal of the developed HA-GQDs-MIP probe showed linearity from 1.0 to 100.0 μg L−1 (y = (0.0405 ± 0.0016)x + (1.20 ± 0.08)) with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.992 (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated based on the IUPAC method: 3SD/slope and 10SD/slope were 0.35 μg L−1 and 1.18 μg L−1, respectively. This result implied that the developed nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe can be used to detect norfloxacin at low concentrations.

The mechanism by which the fluorescence of HA-GQDs-MIP probes was quenched by norfloxacin was described by the Stern-Volmer relationship according to equation [11],
F 0 / F = 1 +
K SV
X
in which F0 and F are intensity without and with norfloxacin, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and [X] is the concentration of norfloxacin. The imprinting factor (IF) was calculated from the ratio of Ksv (MIP) and Ksv (NIP). The IF of the nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP probe was 68.7, which indicated that the probe had excellent specific recognition sites for norfloxacin. The absorption spectrum of norfloxacin did not overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the HA-GQDs-MIP probe (Fig. S5). Since an absence of spectral overlap is normal in cases of electron transfer, the quenching mechanism between the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe and norfloxacin was considered to be based on electron transfer.

Six nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probes, fabricated by the same procedure at different times, exhibited good reproducibility with an RSD of 3.0% (n = 6). The method precision was investigated within a day (n = 3) and among days (n = 6), by analysis of norfloxacin in spiked deionized water at 1.0 μg L−1. The RSD value ranged from 1.5% to 5.5% and from 2.2% to 6.4% in terms of intra-day and inter-day precision, respectively. The developed fluorescent probe also exhibited good stability with emission intensity still above 98% after 3 h of application. The stability of the solid powder of HA-GQDs-MIP was also investigated by keeping it in a desiccator at 25 °C and the fluorescence intensity showed no significant changes after 3 months.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe for norfloxacin detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the HA-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe toward norfloxacin was investigated by detecting norfloxacin and other antibiotics that have similar chemical structures to norfloxacin. These antibiotics were lomefloxacin, levofloxacin, ofloxacin, and ciprofloxacin. The nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe exhibited the highest sensitivity for norfloxacin and sensitivity toward the other compounds was low (Fig. 6 A). The results indicated that the specific recognition cavities of the HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe were highly complementary in size, shape and functional groups with norfloxacin. Competitive binding at the HA-GQDs-MIP probe was investigated by detecting norfloxacin in the presence of different ratios of ciprofloxacin. The results showed that sensitivity did not change (Fig. 6B). These results confirmed that the synthesized nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe was highly specific to norfloxacin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of norfloxacin in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP fluorescent probe was utilized to detect norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk samples. Norfloxacin was found in chicken meat at a concentration of 1.02 μg kg−1 which is below the MRL of some fluoroquinolones antibiotic, MRL of norfloxacin has not yet been fixed. The accuracy of the optosensor was assessed by the detection of norfloxacin spiked in chicken meat and milk samples at 1.0, 10.0, 25.0, and 50.0 μg kg−1. Recoveries were in the range of 93.8 to 99.3% with RSDs under 6.0% (Table 1 ). The method detection limit (MDL) of norfloxacin in milk and chicken meat were calculated to be 0.14 μg L−1 and 0.70 μg kg−1, respectively. The results of norfloxacin detection in spiked samples using the developed optosensor were compared with the results of detection by HPLC. Both methods exhibited a good correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9977 (Fig. S6A), which implied that the developed optosensor could be used for the detection of norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk. Fig. S6B shows the HPLC chromatogram of norfloxacin in spiked chicken meat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of norfloxacin using optosensing and other methods
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of norfloxacin using the fabricated optosensor was compared with detection by other methods (Table 2 ). The present method provided a wide linearity and the LOD was much lower than some previous works reported. Recoveries were also better than or comparable to other methods. This optosensor also exhibited good precision, with RSDs better than or comparable with other works. The other advantages of the developed method over chromatographic techniques are simplicity and rapidity. Nor does the technique require expensive instruments and expert operators. These comparisons show that the proposed optosensor has potential as an alternative method for the detection of trace norfloxacin in complex samples.

An optosensing system based on the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of a nanocomposite HA-GQDs-MIP probe was successfully developed. Applied to detect norfloxacin in chicken meat and milk samples, the system was highly sensitive and exhibited good accuracy with recoveries between 93.8% and 99.3%. In addition, the results of optosensing agreed well with the results of HPLC analysis but the developed method is simpler, faster, cheaper and sensitivity was higher. The developed HA-GQDs-MIP probe showed a high specificity to norfloxacin with an imprinting factor of 68.7. This developed optosensing system can be used as an alternative method for the detection of trace norfloxacin in complex samples and can also be modified for the detection of other antibiotics.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Pajaree Donkhampa: Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Piyaluk Nurerk: Methodology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Prince of Songkla University (SCI6202115N-0), Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English assistance. This research was also supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship from Prince of Songkla University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105127.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical cell designed for in situ integrate microextraction and electroanalysis: trace-level determination of norfloxacin in aqueous samples

Biochem. 117 (2012) 35-47. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jinorgbio.2012.08.008.

Jeyadevan, Enhancement of the electrochemical behavior of CuO nanoleaves on MWCNTs/GC composite film modified electrode for determination of norfloxacin, Colloids. Surf. B 102 (2013) 554-561. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2012.08.051.
K. Drlica, Mechanism of fluoroquinolone action, Curr. Opin. Microbiol. 2 (1999) 504-508. https://doi.org/10.1016/S1369-5274(99)00008-9.

Bulatov, An automated magnetic dispersive micro-solid phase extraction in a fluidized reactor for the determination of fluoroquinolones in baby food samples, Anal. Chim.
